data.title: DiamondRock Hospitality maintains 2025 RevPAR growth range of -1% to +1% with strong out-of-room spend trends
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeffrey John Donnelly opened by emphasizing the company's focus on driving outsized free cash flow per share growth and highlighted strategies such as recycling out of low free cash flow yield hotels, reinvesting in higher-yielding assets, and continuing share repurchases when valuation disconnects exist. Donnelly stated, “To achieve this end, the following is what you should expect from DiamondRock, recycling out of low free cash flow yield hotels into higher-yielding investments, capitalizing on opportunities to dispose of assets where buyers see greater value than we do, reinvesting in our assets when and where outsized ROIs exist, not just outsized RevPAR growth, thoughtfully stretching the renovation life cycle... and reinvesting in ourselves through share repurchases when a valuation disconnect exists.”
  • The company completed a $1.5 billion refinancing and extension of its senior unsecured credit facility, increasing its size from $1.2 billion and maintaining the pricing grid. As a result, all debt will be prepayable at any time without cost or penalty, and there will be no debt maturities until 2029.
  • DRH repurchased just under 1.7 million common shares at an average price of $7.46 during the quarter, with 3.6 million shares repurchased year-to-date for $27.3 million. $146.8 million of capacity remains on the repurchase authorization.
  • The Cliffs at L'Auberge project in Sedona is expected to drive a 25 to 50 basis point tailwind to RevPAR growth in 2026, with wedding revenues at the Cliffs projected to more than double over the full year 2024. Donnelly shared, “Despite the elevated revenue disruption from waiting for a certificate of occupancy, transient and group bookings are now accelerating. Wedding revenues at the Cliffs in this partial year are expected to more than double the full year of 2024.”
  • CFO Briony R. Quinn noted, “Comparable RevPAR growth in the second quarter was 0.1%, driven by a 1.1% increase in rate and an 80 basis point decline in occupancy. RevPAR was negatively impacted by approximately 50 basis points due to our ongoing conversion of the Orchards Inn in Sedona to the Cliffs at L'Auberge.” She also highlighted, “Corporate adjusted EBITDA was $90.5 million and adjusted FFO per share was $0.35. Finally, free cash flow per share for the trailing 12 months... increased approximately 4.5% to $0.63 per share.”

Outlook

  • The company is maintaining its full year outlook for RevPAR growth of negative 1% to plus 1%. Donnelly stated, “We are maintaining our full year outlook for RevPAR growth of negative 1% to plus 1%, but we are encouraged by the increased out-of-room spending trends we experienced in Q2 and early Q3.”
  • Total RevPAR growth is now expected to outperform RevPAR growth by 50 basis points in 2025, an increase from the prior assumption of in-line performance.
  • Corporate adjusted EBITDA guidance for 2025 is $275 million to $295 million, up $2.5 million at the midpoint from prior guidance. FFO per share guidance for 2025 is $0.96 to $1.06, up $0.01 at the midpoint.
  • Projected capital expenditures remain unchanged at $85 million to $95 million.
  • For Q3, DRH expects RevPAR to be down in the low single digits, with the toughest comparisons in August.

Financial Results

  • Comparable RevPAR growth in Q2 2025 was 0.1%. Total RevPAR growth was 1.1% due to a 4.2% increase in out-of-room revenues per occupied room, which reached a quarterly high of $160 per occupied room.
  • Food and beverage revenues increased 3.1%, with F&B profit growing over 6% and margins rising 105 basis points.
  • Operating expenses increased 0.7% (excluding a property tax increase in Chicago), while full portfolio expense growth was 2.6%. Hotel EBITDA margins contracted 97 basis points, but would have increased 30 basis points excluding the Chicago tax increase.
  • Corporate adjusted EBITDA was $90.5 million and adjusted FFO per share was $0.35. Free cash flow per share for the trailing 12 months increased 4.5% to $0.63.
  • Urban portfolio achieved 3% RevPAR growth, while resort portfolio comparable RevPAR declined 6.3%. The delayed opening of the Cliffs at L'Auberge impacted resort results.

Q&A

  • Smedes Rose, Citi, questioned the stabilization at the higher end of the portfolio. CEO Donnelly clarified the comment referred to the overall portfolio, stating, “we are moving towards stabilization. So bottom line, it was meant to be a comment speaking about fundamentals improving from sort of a softer point in time.”
  • Rose also asked about Q3 RevPAR declines—COO Justin L. Leonard attributed this to difficult comps, particularly due to the Democratic National Convention in Chicago last year, not a change in trend.
  • Cooper R. Clark, Wells Fargo, inquired about share buybacks and the preferred stock. CEO Donnelly responded that buybacks remain attractive and the preferred redemption is not in 2025 guidance but will be considered as the year progresses.
  • Clark asked about Sedona's repositioning; Donnelly said forward bookings for the Cliffs show encouraging group business and rate increases of $150 to $200 year-over-year.
  • Austin Todd Wurschmidt, KeyBanc, sought detail on group pace improvements. CEO Donnelly said most improvement is in urban hotels, with Q4 group optimism due to more available booking weeks. Boston is expected to be the strongest group market in 2026.
  • Chris Jon Woronka, Deutsche Bank, raised the issue of potential competition from cruise lines in resort markets. Donnelly said it is considered, but difficult to isolate its impact.
  • Duane Thomas Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI, asked about industry RevPAR and refinancing impact. Donnelly expressed hope for RevPAR acceleration in 2026, while CFO Quinn highlighted increased flexibility from the recent refinancing.
  • Daniel Patrick Hogan, Baird, questioned sustainability of out-of-room spend growth. Donnelly said it's too early to predict for 2026 and believes price is a bigger driver than volume.
  • Chris Darling, Green Street, asked about asset sales and portfolio size. Donnelly said asset sales will be balanced with capital recycling and that being a smaller REIT is not a major concern.
  • Floris Van Dijkum, Ladenburg, discussed valuation metrics. Donnelly advocated for incorporating after CapEx metrics like free cash flow per share into valuation.
  • Kenneth G. Billingsley, Compass Point, asked about group business and competition. Management said large hotels rely on brand channels while independents focus on smaller, high-dollar groups. Billingsley also asked about disposition hurdles, to which Donnelly cited tax and credit market volatility, but said no current obstacles remain.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about Q3 RevPAR declines, group booking pace, and asset disposition timing, with a tone that was generally neutral to slightly negative.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone, with Donnelly stating, “our operating results and forward bookings indicate we are possibly entering a more stable operating environment than we were experiencing just 3 months ago.” Management was more upbeat in prepared remarks and cautious but reassured during Q&A.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sounded more constructive about stabilization but remained mindful of macro risks and comps.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The full year RevPAR growth guidance remained unchanged at -1% to +1%, but management now expects total RevPAR growth to outperform RevPAR growth by 50 basis points in 2025, an upgrade from the prior in-line assumption.
  • Corporate adjusted EBITDA guidance midpoint increased by $2.5 million and FFO per share midpoint by $0.01 versus Q1.
  • The Q2 call highlighted increased out-of-room spending, improved share repurchase activity, and successful refinancing, while the Q1 call focused more on cost controls, group booking uncertainties, and the impact of calendar shifts.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from renovation costs and group conversion pauses in Q1 to Q3 comps and asset disposition timing in Q2. Management’s tone was incrementally more confident about stabilization and future growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macro uncertainty, challenging comps from one-time events in 2024, and delays in asset dispositions due to regulatory and credit market volatility.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of out-of-room spend growth and the competitive landscape for group business.
  • Mitigation strategies include cost controls, share repurchases, maintaining balance sheet flexibility, and focusing on high-ROI renovations and asset recycling.

Final Takeaway

DiamondRock Hospitality’s Q2 2025 call reinforced its focus on disciplined capital allocation, with continued share repurchases, a strengthened balance sheet, and targeted asset renovations expected to drive free cash flow per share growth. Management maintained its RevPAR growth outlook and signaled confidence in stabilization trends, particularly in group demand and out-of-room revenue, while remaining attentive to macroeconomic headwinds and competitive pressures in both urban and resort portfolios.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump removes IRS chief Billy Long after just two months - report
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump is ousting Internal Revenue Service Commisioner Billy Long just two months into the job, according to a media report published Friday.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is stepping in as acting IRS chief until a permanent replacement is set, the New York Times reported, citing four people familiar with the matter.

Long is anticipated to be nominated to an ambassadorship, the people added.

The move comes as the Trump administration has slashed IRS staffing while aiming to use the regulator to target migrants and probe universities' tax-exempt status, the article said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Eton targets $80M revenue run-rate in Q3 2025 as product launches accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eton Pharmaceuticals (ETON) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sean E. Brynjelsen stated that "it was another record quarter for the company" and described 2025 as "the most exciting time in our history." He highlighted strong momentum from recent acquisitions and portfolio growth, noting the company is outperforming expectations with three product launches planned for the year.
  • Brynjelsen reported "record product sales and more than 100% revenue growth year-over-year," driven by the relaunches of Increlex and Galzin, as well as FDA approval and rapid launch of Khindivi. He also announced the NDA submission for ET-600, which has a PDUFA date of February 2026, setting up another potential near-term launch.
  • The CEO detailed that Increlex reached its goal of 100 active patients five months ahead of schedule, and the company is now targeting 110 by year-end, with a long-term goal of 185 patients representing a potential $50M–$60M in annual sales. He outlined a strategy to harmonize U.S. and EU diagnostic criteria for Increlex, which could grow the addressable market to over 1,000 patients and expand annual sales potential to nearly $300M if successful.
  • Brynjelsen described Khindivi’s launch as "moving along... additive" to Alkindi Sprinkle, with the two expected to surpass $50M in combined sales in the coming years. He acknowledged a near-term headwind due to an age restriction on Khindivi’s label but said a revised formulation has been developed and a meeting with the FDA is scheduled to enable a broader label by the end of 2026.
  • The CEO noted Galzin’s transition to the Eton Cares support program, solving historical access and affordability challenges, and set a goal to reach 200 active patients by year-end. He described plans for ET-700, an extended-release version of Galzin, as a "very large market opportunity" with a proof-of-concept study planned for year-end.
  • Brynjelsen said the company’s financial position is "unique," with more than $30M in cash and continued discipline in pursuing acquisitions that fit its rare disease strategy.
  • CFO James R. Gruber reported that "second quarter revenue was $18.9 million compared to $9.1 million in the second quarter of 2024, an increase of 108% and was comprised entirely of product sales." He emphasized that "we now expect to achieve an $80 million annual revenue run rate in the third quarter of 2025, one quarter ahead of previous projections."

Outlook

  • Management now expects to reach the $80M revenue run-rate in Q3 2025, three months ahead of prior guidance. Sequential revenue growth is anticipated to continue, but "it will not be at as rapid of a pace as was seen from Q1 to Q2." The company expects to report full-year 2025 adjusted gross margin of approximately 70% and for long-term adjusted gross margin to reach 75% by 2028.
  • The company remains confident in reaching $50M in combined Alkindi Sprinkle and Khindivi sales with expansion plans once Khindivi’s label is broadened. The upcoming launch of ET-600 in early 2026 is projected to further enhance growth prospects.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the quarter was $18.9M, up from $9.1M in the prior-year period, driven by contributions from Alkindi Sprinkle, Increlex, and Galzin.
  • Gross profit was $11.9M, and adjusted gross profit was $14.1M or 75% of revenue, reflecting a higher margin portfolio mix. Adjusted EBITDA was $3.1M, a turnaround from negative $1.6M a year earlier.
  • Operating cash flow reached $8M, with cash on hand reported at $25.4M at quarter end and "more than $30 million" as of the call, including a payment from Esteve for Increlex rights.
  • R&D expenses totaled $3.7M, including a $2.2M NDA application fee and $0.5M licensing payment. Adjusted G&A expenses were $7.6M, with management expecting these costs to remain flat or decline in the second half of the year.
  • Net loss was ($2.6M) compared to ($3.0M) prior year; non-GAAP net income was $1.5M vs. a loss of ($1.9M) a year ago.

Q&A

  • Chase Richard Knickerbocker, Craig-Hallum: Asked about early Khindivi launch momentum, patient volume outlook, and product breakout. Brynjelsen responded that Khindivi’s adoption has been strong among patients 5 and up and is "more additive than anything else" to Alkindi. He declined to break out product-specific numbers but stated the combined franchise is "very clearly on track to do more than $50 million."
  • Knickerbocker: Asked about Increlex trial design for FDA label expansion. Brynjelsen explained the plan for an open-label IND study enrolling patients with IGF levels between -2 and -3 SD, aiming to expand access and collect data for a future FDA submission.
  • Madison Britt Wynne El-Saadi, B. Riley: Inquired about the source of revenue outperformance across brands. Gruber confirmed the beat was "definitely heavily weighted toward Increlex," with growth in Alkindi and Galzin as well.
  • El-Saadi: Sought clarity on FDA feedback for Increlex label expansion. Brynjelsen indicated the FDA requested a protocol for an open IND, and consultants are "confident that, that would be acceptable to the Agency."
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about the Khindivi age label expansion pathway and potential patient numbers. Brynjelsen explained a revised formulation is ready, with a submission expected in the first half of 2026 and targeted approval by end of 2026. He projected the combined franchise could reach "probably 2,000 patients on product."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a positive tone, congratulating management on the quarter and probing for details on growth drivers and regulatory progress, with frequent references to outperformance and future catalysts.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we remain confident" and "the future has never been brighter for Eton." There was openness regarding strategic priorities and regulatory plans.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased confidence, with more focus on revenue outperformance and acceleration of growth timelines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised, with the $80M revenue run-rate now expected in Q3 rather than Q4. The tone shifted to highlight overperformance and accelerated timelines for product launches and regulatory milestones.
  • The focus shifted from initial launches and regulatory preparations in Q1 to execution, patient growth, and market expansion in Q2.
  • Analysts’ questions evolved from regulatory processes and initial adoption to specific drivers of outperformance and detailed execution strategies.
  • Management displayed greater certainty, citing tangible achievements such as surpassing patient targets and accelerating timelines for both regulatory filings and market expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • The main short-term risk is the limited age label on Khindivi, which management is addressing with a new formulation and FDA engagement.
  • Increlex’s long-term growth depends on successful harmonization of U.S. and EU diagnostic criteria and execution of a follow-on study.
  • Gross margin is anticipated to decline in the second half of 2025 due to a new international distribution model for Increlex, but management expects margins to recover longer-term.
  • No new material risks or regulatory setbacks were highlighted.

Final Takeaway

Eton Pharmaceuticals delivered a record second quarter, with revenue growth and patient numbers surpassing internal targets. Management now expects to achieve an $80M annual revenue run-rate in Q3, driven by strong launches of Increlex, Galzin, and Khindivi, while laying the groundwork for broader label expansions and additional product launches in 2026. The company remains focused on disciplined growth, margin expansion, and addressing regulatory hurdles to unlock further value across its rare disease portfolio.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: INmune Bio outlines CORDStrom BLA and MAA filings targeted for mid-2026 as CEO transition highlights strategic shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: INmune Bio (INMB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • David J. Moss announced his appointment as Chief Executive Officer following the retirement of co-founder RJ Tesi, stating, "I'm honored to address you today as the new Chief Executive Officer of INmune Bio...I'm excited to step into this role and build on the strong foundation he has laid." Moss emphasized the company's focus on three platforms: XPro for Alzheimer's, CORDStrom for Recessive Dystrophic Epidermolysis Bullosa (RDEB), and INKmune in oncology.
  • Moss reported, "The data confirms that patients with Alzheimer's disease who exhibit 2 or more biomarkers of inflammation are the optimal candidates for XPro...These findings reinforce our hypothesis that XPro is uniquely suited to address Alzheimer's disease patients with elevated inflammation levels." He highlighted ongoing preliminary discussions for strategic partnerships and noted an emphasis on rare disease pathways for faster market access.
  • Moss confirmed that the company "has made the decision to not pursue treatment-resistant depression at this time" for XPro, shifting focus to more efficient indications.
  • Regarding CORDStrom, Moss stated, "We anticipate filing for approval in both jurisdictions by midyear 2026."
  • On INKmune, Moss said, "Our Phase I/II program is near completion...the data demonstrate that INKmune is safe and delivers immunologic benefits," with plans to explore earlier-stage disease trials.
  • Moss introduced Cory Ellspermann as the new Interim CFO, expressing "full confidence and support in Cory and his ability to help take INmune forward."
  • CFO Moss reported, "Net loss attributable to common stockholders for the quarter ended June 30, 2025, was approximately $24.5 million compared with approximately $9.7 million for the comparable period in '24." He added, "As of June 30, 2025, the company had cash and cash equivalents of approximately $33.4 million. Based on our current operating plan, we believe our cash is sufficient to fund operations into Q3 of 2026."

Outlook

  • Moss reiterated, "We expect to have the manuscript and the peer-reviewed publication filed on the MINDFuL trial sometime this month...We expect to have the end of Phase II meeting with the FDA to take place sometime in Q4 of this year."
  • For CORDStrom, "the company is working vigorously on the CORDStrom marketing authorization application and the biologics licensing application to file by midyear or earlier 2026."
  • Moss highlighted that strategic partnerships are being pursued but are dependent on the outcome of the FDA end of Phase II meeting for XPro, stating, "I don't expect the partnership to occur until after we've had...the end of Phase II meeting with the FDA."

Financial Results

  • Moss reported, "Net loss attributable to common stockholders for the quarter ended June 30, 2025, was approximately $24.5 million compared with approximately $9.7 million for the comparable period in '24. Research and development expense totaled approximately $5.8 million...General and administrative expenses were approximately $2.3 million." He noted, "Impairment of acquired in-process research and development intangible assets was $16.5 million compared with 0 during the comparable period in 2024."
  • The company "has decided to take a very conservative approach and to halt immediate plans to further develop XPro and AD at this time, given the cost of a Phase III program as it seeks partnerships...and wrote off the value of XPro's intangible asset value."
  • Cash and cash equivalents at quarter-end totaled $33.4 million, with operations funded into Q3 of 2026.

Q&A

  • Denis Reznik, Raymond James: Asked about the timing of the FDA meeting for XPro and changes in agency contacts. Moss responded, "We have not yet filed a briefing book with the FDA...It should fall sometime November, December if we get it in on our target date."
  • Reznik followed up on feedback at AAIC and partnership prospects. Barnum highlighted strong industry feedback, particularly on neuropsychiatric inventory improvements and safety profile: "the feedback...was pretty obvious that this is the appropriate subgroup...the lack of ARIA...is something that the field is really looking for."
  • Jenny Shen, BTIG: Inquired about EMACC as a biomarker and NPI as a registrational endpoint. Barnum expressed confidence in EMACC's performance and anticipated FDA receptivity.
  • James Molloy, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about partnership timing and EMACC feedback. Barnum confirmed no partnership is imminent before FDA consultation and noted supportive scientific consensus on EMACC.
  • Boris Tolkachev, Freedom Broker: Questioned statistical analysis improvements for CORDStrom and open-label trial details. Lowdell explained enhancements in statistical analysis and outlined plans for an open-label trial post-BLA submission.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed detailed, forward-looking questions about regulatory timing, partnership prospects, biomarker selection, and trial design, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity on execution and milestones.
  • Management maintained a confident and transparent tone in prepared remarks, but adopted a measured stance regarding partnership expectations and regulatory pathways. Moss stated, "I don't expect the partnership to occur until after we've had...the end of Phase II meeting with the FDA."
  • Compared to the prior quarter, analysts’ tone remained inquisitive, while management shifted from more optimistic projections to caution and strategic conservatism, particularly regarding XPro's development.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The CEO role transitioned from RJ Tesi to David J. Moss, marking a significant change in leadership.
  • Strategic focus shifted, with XPro’s immediate development halted pending partnerships and greater emphasis placed on CORDStrom’s regulatory filings and INKmune’s safety data.
  • Management tone moved from high anticipation for MINDFuL trial results in Q1 to a more cautious, data-driven outlook in Q2, as seen in Moss's statements about conservative financial actions and partnership dependencies.
  • Analysts continued to focus on regulatory, partnership, and trial design issues, but the shift in management’s messaging from rapid advancement to resource-conscious progression was clear.

Risks and Concerns

  • Moss cited the high cost of a Phase III program for XPro in Alzheimer’s disease as a barrier, leading to a halt in immediate development without a partnership.
  • An impairment charge of $16.5 million was taken as the company wrote off XPro’s intangible asset value.
  • Dependence on securing strategic partnerships and nondilutive funding was emphasized as necessary for advancing pipeline programs.
  • Regulatory approval timelines for CORDStrom and INKmune hinge on successful submissions and adequate capital.

Final Takeaway

INmune Bio’s Q2 2025 call highlighted a pivotal phase marked by new leadership, a strategic pause in XPro’s immediate Alzheimer’s development, and the advancement of CORDStrom toward BLA and MAA filings targeted for mid-2026. Management underscored the importance of data-driven decisions, prudent financial management, and the pursuit of partnerships to support its pipeline’s progression, with a strengthened focus on regulatory submissions and efficient resource allocation through the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fox Factory narrows EPS guidance to $1.60–$2 as tariff headwinds intensify and sales outlook rises to $1.51B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fox Factory Holding Corp. (FOXF) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michael C. Dennison indicated the company delivered “solid progress in the second quarter with $375 million in net sales, representing growth across all 3 segments.” He highlighted that “consolidated adjusted EBITDA margin continued to improve this quarter to 13.1%, which is the highest level for Fox Factory in nearly 2 years.”
  • Dennison detailed four key initiatives: footprint consolidation (“benefits from our Taiwan consolidation are now flowing through the P&L”), portfolio optimization (“targeted improvements to our product mix... across all product lines”), working capital management (“continued improvements in our supply chain practices and inventory optimization... paving the way for enhanced cash flow”), and cost reduction (“our $25 million cost reduction program is delivering on schedule with approximately 30% of the benefits realized so far”).
  • The CEO said, “We remain a growth company at our core. And as the consumer discretionary environment stabilizes, we expect this combination of operational excellence and innovation-led growth, particularly in our higher-margin product lines to support the enhanced profitability we've been methodically building toward.”
  • CFO Dennis Charles Schemm stated, “Total consolidated net sales in the second quarter of fiscal 2025 were $374.9 million, an increase of 7.6% versus the same quarter last year, reflecting solid growth in all 3 segments.” Schemm also noted, “Net income for the second quarter of fiscal 2025 was $2.7 million or $0.07 per diluted share compared to net income of $5.4 million or $0.13 per diluted share in the same period last year.”

Outlook

  • Schemm reported third quarter expectations: “net sales in the range of $370 million to $390 million and adjusted earnings per diluted share in the range of $0.45 to $0.65.”
  • For the full year, Schemm announced, “we are increasing our sales guidance from the original range of $1.385 billion to $1.485 billion to an updated range that brackets the high end at $1.45 billion to $1.51 billion.” He added, “we are adjusting earnings per diluted share to $1.60 to $2, which narrows the original range of $1.60 to $2.60 towards the lower end, reflecting the incremental unmitigated tariff pressure we have discussed.”
  • Management described a “pre-mitigated tariff impact of upwards of $50 million for the full year 2025,” with expectations to “offset approximately 50% of our full year tariff costs through various countermeasures.”

Financial Results

  • Adjusted gross margin was reported at 31.3% compared to 31.9% in the prior year period, with sequential increases of 30 to 40 basis points supported by cost reduction initiatives.
  • Adjusted EBITDA increased 11.8% year-over-year to $49.3 million for the quarter, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 13.1%, marking a sequential increase of 190 basis points from Q1 2025.
  • Working capital as a percentage of sales improved by 80 basis points sequentially to 30.7%.
  • As of July 3, 2025, the company held a revolver balance of $157 million and a term loan balance of $517.5 million, with net leverage reduced to 3.8x and a target of below 3x by year-end. Fox Factory expects to generate approximately $80 million in free cash flow for the full year.

Q&A

  • Lawrence Scott Solow, CJS Securities: Asked if raised sales guidance was driven by any particular segment and the prospects for top-line growth. Dennison responded that “no significant change by any one segment or business... it’s really the entire enterprise delivering on our commitments.”
  • Solow further inquired about top-line initiatives. Dennison emphasized, “the reason why we're bucking the trend... is a clear function of focus on that top line and product. We haven't lost focus with that at all. In fact, we probably doubled down on that.”
  • Bret David Jordan, Jefferies: Questioned the increased tariff impact and its distribution. Schemm explained, “I'd put AAG rate at $10 million, Marucci at $15 million and PVG at $25 million.”
  • Jordan also asked about powersports outlook. Dennison remarked, “there is more stability in powersports now than there was probably 6 months ago, 9 months ago. And we see inventories balancing.”
  • Scott Lewis Stember, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about on-road truck and Marucci performance. Dennison clarified on-road automotive business remains “very stable,” and stated, “Marucci will be up this year, and it will actually be a record year.”
  • Peter Clement McGoldrick, Stifel: Sought clarity on bike segment growth and inventory normalization. Dennison described 2025 as “a growth year for bike for us. This is also a stability year for bike for us.”
  • Alexander Thomas Perry, BofA Securities: Queried about the EPS guide reduction. Schemm confirmed, “we are really running after this tariff from a mitigation perspective.... This company is trying to control what it can control, and that has been that story all along with us is delevering, getting after working capital, taking cost out, mitigating these tariffs as much as we can.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on segment growth drivers, tariff impacts, and the durability of margin and revenue improvements, signaling a slightly cautious and probing tone.
  • Management maintained confidence during prepared remarks and the Q&A, though responses around tariffs and EPS guidance reflected measured caution. Dennison repeated, “we are really running after this tariff from a mitigation perspective,” and emphasized product innovation as a growth lever.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone remained probing with a focus on how macro and tariff headwinds were being managed, while management’s tone shifted from optimistic resolve to cautious confidence, particularly regarding tariff mitigation and narrowed EPS guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sales guidance increased to $1.51 billion at the high end, compared to the prior quarter’s $1.485 billion, driven by first-half outperformance.
  • EPS guidance narrowed to $1.60–$2, whereas the previous quarter’s range extended to $2.60, reflecting incremental tariff headwinds.
  • Management’s tone, while confident, is now more focused on risk mitigation and operational discipline than the optimism of Q1. Analysts remained focused on segment specifics, tariff impacts, and the trajectory of product innovation and market share gains.
  • Tariff headwinds have grown more pronounced, with management detailing mitigation strategies and the updated estimate for tariff impact.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff-driven cost inflation remains a critical challenge, with management reporting a pre-mitigated impact of up to $50 million for 2025.
  • Margin pressures are persistent, especially in segments exposed to tariffs, such as Marucci and PVG.
  • Management’s mitigation strategies include in-sourcing, relocating manufacturing, cost-sharing arrangements with OEM partners, and selective price increases, but not all incremental costs are expected to be fully offset in the short term.

Final Takeaway

Fox Factory management reinforced that sales momentum and operational improvements have enabled an upward revision to full-year revenue guidance. However, persistent and intensifying tariff headwinds are expected to pressure margins, leading to a tightening of the EPS outlook to the lower end of the previous range. Management continues to prioritize cost controls, product innovation, and working capital efficiency as key levers to offset external challenges and position the company for long-term growth and enhanced profitability as markets stabilize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Why mid-caps could be the way to go, shifting from tech giants – Greenwich’s CIO
data.description:
Investors should consider shifting away from large-cap tech stocks (XLK), (MAGS) toward mid-cap (SP400), (NYSEARCA:SPMD) companies for better value in today’s market, according to Vahan Janjigian, chief investment officer at Greenwich Wealth Management.

In an interview with CNBC, Janjigian pointed out that the S&P 500 (SP500) can no longer be considered a broad-based market index as the top 10 stocks (NVDA), (MSFT), (AAPL), (AMZN), (META), (AVGO), (GOOGL), (GOOG), (TSLA), (BRK.B) account for 40% of the weight, while the top half of stocks make up 90% of the index.

“For the past hundred years, until about February 2008, value (IVE) has outperformed growth (IVW) and small cap (IWM), (SP600) has outperformed large cap (SP500),” he said.

He noted that the 2008 financial crisis and subsequent Federal Reserve intervention created unprecedented market conditions where traditional investment approaches stopped working as expected.

The current market shows signs of euphoria, and the U.S. market’s cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio higher than it has been 98% of the time. Janjigian attributes much of this excitement to younger investors following momentum strategies, saying “a lot of investors [are] looking at what’s working and just piling in and driving that higher.”

When asked about tariff impacts, Janjigian suggested that while mid-cap companies typically generate more of their revenue domestically, they aren’t necessarily less exposed to tariffs since they still rely on foreign manufacturers. “I think investors have reached that conclusion, and they believe that the larger companies can handle it better and perhaps absorb more of the cost,” he said.

Despite advocating for mid-cap diversification, Janjigian highlighted Verizon (NYSE:VZ) as a large-cap value stock he particularly likes. “This is a stock that pays a very generous dividend. It’s been increasing the dividend every single year for the past 18 years and – believe it or not – it’s actually outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) so far this year,” he said, contrasting Verizon’s reasonable 9x earnings valuation with growth stocks like Palantir (PLTR) trading at approximately 285x earnings.

“I do believe it makes sense to pay more for growth, but the big question is how much more should you pay for growth?” he concluded, suggesting that patient investors might find better value in stocks like Verizon (NYSE:VZ) rather than chasing momentum in the current market environment.

data.title: PTC Therapeutics outlines Sephience $1B-plus U.S. revenue potential following FDA and EU launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PTC Therapeutics (PTCT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Matthew B. Klein opened the call highlighting the "first approvals for Sephience for the treatment of children and adults with PKU" and described Sephience as "the foundational product for PTC's sustainable growth and path to profitability." Klein stated, "We again had solid revenue performance with total revenue of $179 million, with continued contributions from our DMD franchise, including in Europe."
  • Klein emphasized, "We expect Sephience to be the foundational product for PTC's sustainable growth and path to profitability," and noted the EU and FDA approvals with broad labeling across disease subtypes and age groups. He added, "Based on the strong Sephience data package and the significant unmet need for PKU patients, Sephience is positioned to become the new standard of care for children and adults living with PKU."
  • Klein indicated, "We believe the Sephience revenue opportunity in the U.S. exceeds $1 billion, and our global customer-facing teams are excited to bring this therapy to all those that could benefit."
  • Klein described a strategic transaction: "PTC has reached an agreement to purchase the annual global net sales payment obligation of 8% to 12% that was part of the acquisition of Censa Pharmaceuticals in 2020. PTC will pay the participating Censa rights holders approximately $225 million upfront and additional future sales milestones for approximately 90% of our net sales payment obligation."
  • Chief Business Officer Eric Pauwels reported, "We generated $96 million revenue in the second quarter from our global DMD franchise." Pauwels added, "Our experienced U.S. neurology team is ready to bring Translarna to nonsense mutation DMD patients following potential FDA approval."
  • Chief Financial Officer Pierre Gravier stated, "Total product, collaboration and royalty revenue for the second quarter was $179 million, including DMD franchise revenue of $96 million."
  • Gravier highlighted the company’s "strong financial position, closing the quarter with approximately $1.99 billion in cash, allowing us to fully support all planned commercial and R&D initiatives, engage in strategic business development activities and achieve cash flow breakeven without the need for additional capital."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed full-year 2025 revenue guidance of $650 million to $800 million. Gravier stated, "Guidance, $650 million to $800 million, as we discussed, the bulk of it is our existing product portfolio and obviously includes new product launch as well."
  • Klein commented on European Translarna revenue: "For the remainder of 2025, we expect to be able to maintain approximately 25% of European revenue from prior to authorization nonrenewal."
  • Pauwels pointed to growth plans: "We are equally excited about the anticipated regulatory approvals of Sephience in Japan and Brazil before the end of the year."

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for the second quarter was $179 million.
  • DMD franchise revenue was $96 million, with Translarna net product revenue at $59 million and Emflaza net product revenue at $36 million.
  • Royalty revenue from Evrysdi (Roche) was $58 million for the quarter.
  • Non-GAAP R&D expense was $104 million (excluding $9 million in noncash stock-based compensation), and non-GAAP SG&A expense was $76 million (excluding $10 million in noncash stock-based compensation).
  • Cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities totaled $1,989 million as of June 30, 2025.

Q&A

  • Kristen Brianne Kluska, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about Huntington's trial design discussions with FDA and the sustainability of European Translarna revenue. Klein explained the plan is "to come away with alignment for what the key elements of that efficacy trial will be" and clarified that Article 117 revenue continuation is "at the discretion of individual countries."
  • Tazeen Ahmad, Bank of America: Inquired about early Sephience launch metrics. Pauwels responded, "The metrics we're going to continually provide on a quarterly basis will be prescriptions, patient start forms, the number of commercial patients that are currently available on treatment."
  • Lut Ming Cheng, JPMorgan: Asked about payer contracting for Sephience. Pauwels said, "We see right now that the clinical profile of Sephience is being very well received...Importantly, very minimal restrictions."
  • Judah Frommer, Morgan Stanley: Sought clarification on the Censa payment transaction. Gravier said, "This is a very high return on capital."
  • Dingding Shi, Jefferies: Questioned PKU launch sequencing and guidance. Klein stated, "somewhere around 7,000 patients fit into that first wave of the sequence."
  • Multiple analysts raised questions about regulatory timelines for vatiquinone and Translarna, with Klein stating that both NDAs remain under FDA review and providing updates on discussions but no specific action dates.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment on Sephience launch and financial performance, with several congratulatory remarks and a focus on early launch momentum and revenue guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, especially when discussing Sephience adoption and revenue potential, as seen in statements like "We are thrilled with the recent FDA approval of Sephience" and "We are confident we will maintain a very close and narrow pricing corridor."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts exhibited increased optimism, particularly on Sephience's prospects and operational readiness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sephience received both FDA and EU approval this quarter, while in Q1 approval was pending.
  • Revenue from the DMD franchise declined from $134 million in Q1 to $96 million in Q2.
  • Total revenue decreased from $190 million in Q1 to $179 million in Q2.
  • Strategic focus shifted more heavily toward Sephience launch and monetization, while DMD franchise and Emflaza faced continued erosion and loss of European authorization for Translarna.
  • Management’s confidence in Sephience as a growth driver increased, with more detailed commentary on launch dynamics and market access.
  • Guidance for full-year revenue remained unchanged at $650 million to $800 million, consistent with the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued market erosion for Emflaza due to generic entrants.
  • Potential uncertainty regarding the sustainability of Article 117-based Translarna revenue in Europe, as it depends on individual country decisions and may be revisited periodically.
  • Ongoing regulatory risk for vatiquinone and Translarna NDAs, with no definitive timelines provided for FDA action.
  • Competition and payer dynamics for Sephience are being monitored, though management reported "minimal restrictions" so far.

Final Takeaway

PTC Therapeutics emphasized the transformative impact of Sephience’s FDA and EU approvals, positioning it as the core growth driver with a U.S. revenue opportunity above $1 billion. The company’s robust financial position supports ongoing launches and R&D, with full-year revenue guidance unchanged. As the launch of Sephience accelerates and new market approvals are pursued, management remains focused on executing its strategy, leveraging its commercial infrastructure, and maintaining operational discipline amid evolving regulatory and market dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: British American Tobacco shares clock nine-session winning streak
data.description:
British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) shares clocked nine straight sessions of gains, as the stock rose 0.8% at $57.12 on Friday.

The tobacco products company gained 7.4% in the preceding eight sessions. The stock has surged 57% so far this year, compared to a near 8% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

BTI is up 12% over the past one month.

Most recently, BTI backed its full-year guidance after disclosing solid first-half results. The tobacco giant now expects to reach the upper end of its 2025 forecasts for revenue growth of 1% to 2% at constant rates and adjusted operational profit growth between 1.5% and 2.5%. 

"While BTI's dividend yield is now lower than some competitors, it's still healthy on its own. Also, the valuation and growth in new categories support a continued positive outlook," pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, BTI has a Hold rating with a score of 3.2 out of 5. The company received A+ in the prospect of profitability and momentum, while it got a D growth factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, two analysts gave BTI a Strong Buy rating. Two analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, while one recommended Strong Sell.

 
 
 

Seeking Alpha analysts are positive and see the stock as a Buy.

data.title: Globus Medical reaffirms $2.8B–$2.9B 2025 sales outlook as integration advances and U.S. Spine momentum builds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Globus Medical (GMED) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Keith W. Pfeil, CEO, opened his first earnings call by emphasizing continuity in Globus Medical’s strategy and mission, stating the company remains focused on innovation, patient outcomes, and financial discipline. Pfeil highlighted, “Our strategy remains grounded in the same principles that have driven our success for many years. Our mission remains unchanged. Globus Medical is a global musculoskeletal technology company dedicated to improving clinical outcomes and solving unmet clinical needs to improve the lives of our patients.”
  • Pfeil noted that the company is finalizing integration of recent acquisitions, including Nevro, and is accelerating product development, particularly with the DuraPro drill system, which has seen “accelerated levels of growth in this product since launch, fueled by surgeon-to-surgeon testimonials.”
  • He cited strong commercial performance in the U.S. Spine business, with 19 consecutive weeks of implant growth, and announced the launch of the ONVOY Acetabular Shell and FDA clearance for Excelsius XR, “our head-maned augmented reality navigation headset.” Pfeil stated, “We look forward to bringing this exciting innovative technology to surgeons in the coming months.”
  • Pfeil also introduced Kyle Kline as the new CFO, recognizing his “trusted confidence” and experience within the organization.
  • CFO Kyle Kline highlighted, “We are extremely pleased with our second quarter results, both with and without the impact of Nevro. The headlines for the quarter include: one, above-market sales growth in our U.S. Spine business; two, record non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.86; and three, a bounce back in enabling technologies sales.”

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its 2025 net sales guidance of $2.8 billion to $2.9 billion and fully diluted non-GAAP earnings per share between $3 and $3.30. Pfeil confirmed, “We are reaffirming our guide of 2025 net sales to be in the range of $2.8 billion to $2.9 billion and fully diluted non-GAAP earnings per share between $3 and $3.30.”
  • Kline reiterated confidence in the guidance, indicating no current plans to adjust it up or down after a strong Q2, stating, “We feel comfortable in the range of our guidance and where we sit today.”
  • Management noted that ongoing manufacturing initiatives are expected to drive a return to mid-70s adjusted gross profit profile over time.

Financial Results

  • Second quarter revenue was $745.3 million, up 18.4% year over year, with non-GAAP EPS of $0.86, up 14.1%. Free cash flow reached $31.3 million.
  • GAAP net income was $202.8 million, or $1.49 per share, reflecting a bargain purchase gain from the Nevro acquisition and offset by merger-related costs.
  • Legacy Globus sales totaled $650.8 million, up 3.3%, while Nevro contributed $94.6 million in revenue.
  • U.S. revenue grew to $600.8 million, up 20.3%, led by the U.S. Spine business, which achieved its highest growth rate since the NuVasive merger.
  • Enabling Technologies revenue was $35.2 million, showing a 58% sequential increase but a 4% year-over-year decline.
  • Adjusted gross profit was 67.4%. The base Globus business delivered adjusted EBITDA margins of 32.3%.
  • Cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities stood at $229.4 million at quarter-end, with a recently expanded $500 million share repurchase program.

Q&A

  • Matthew Stephan Miksic, Barclays: Asked about Nevro integration and cost actions. Pfeil responded, “Leadership has really surrounded the sales force, number one. They're really energized to be part of the Globus family now...we really overlaid the Globus approach to controlling third-party spending.”
  • Miksic also inquired about enabling technology sales and pipeline. Pfeil explained, “I still see that elongation pipeline. As I think about how robots are sold, we're still selling outright the majority of our robots...I don't see us losing deals to competition.”
  • Vik Chopra, Wells Fargo: Asked about evolving investor communication and augmented reality headset launch. Pfeil stated, “We're in the process of beginning quoting XR. Finished goods supply is ready. And really now it's about rolling out the launch plan.”
  • Shagun Singh Chadha, RBC: Sought details on leadership transitions and Nevro growth strategy. David C. Paul replied, “Our leadership bench has always been broad and deep, and we're always developing leaders...our continuity in leadership is second to none in our industry.” Pfeil added, “We want to bring to Nevro is stability and approach. I commented on really taking and recasting our product development approach.”
  • Matthew Charles Taylor, Jefferies: Asked about Nevro EBITDA and accretion timeline. Kline answered, “Profitability, we do feel good about, but there's a lot we don't know about the business. 3 months in, we've been able to...come out with some synergy actions.”
  • Caitlin Cronin, Canaccord: Sought updates on NuVasive in-sourcing and integration. Pfeil responded, “The manufacturing initiatives are really multifaceted...the cadence of output from our manufacturing facilities will continue to increase as time passes.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a slightly positive tone, congratulating new leadership and focusing on integration execution, future growth from Nevro, and enabling technology momentum. There were probing questions about guidance components, Nevro accretion, and integration timelines, but no overt skepticism or negative sentiment.
  • Management remained confident and forward-looking throughout, frequently using phrases such as “we remain confident,” “we feel comfortable,” and “we are well positioned.” In Q&A, management adopted a measured, optimistic stance, reiterating confidence in ongoing strategies and integration.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased confidence, with less focus on supply chain concerns and more emphasis on growth and integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a transition in executive leadership, with Pfeil moving from CFO to CEO and Kline appointed CFO, both receiving strong endorsements from management.
  • Guidance for 2025 net sales and EPS was reaffirmed, unchanged from Q1. In Q1, the company had lowered EPS guidance slightly to account for Nevro acquisition costs, but no further guidance changes were made in Q2.
  • Operational challenges and supply chain issues flagged in Q1 were reported as largely resolved in Q2, contributing to improved financial performance and sentiment.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from concerns about supply chain and Nevro acquisition risk in Q1 to integration progress, growth initiatives, and upcoming product launches in Q2.
  • Management’s tone evolved from defensive and explanatory in Q1 to more assertive and optimistic in Q2, reflecting operational improvements and a strong sales quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management pointed to elongated sales cycles in enabling technologies but emphasized a robust pipeline and strong product feedback.
  • International revenue growth was described as mixed, with some regions impacted by lingering supply challenges and distributor transitions, particularly in the LatAm region.
  • Integration of acquired businesses remains a key focus, with cost actions underway for Nevro and continued work on combining international operations from NuVasive.
  • Tariff impacts were described as not material, but management continues to monitor and implement cost offsets as needed.

Final Takeaway

Globus Medical’s Q2 2025 call highlighted sustained momentum in U.S. Spine and trauma, successful integration of Nevro, and the launch of innovative products such as the DuraPro drill and Excelsius XR. The company reaffirmed its full-year sales and EPS guidance, citing operational improvements and a robust product pipeline. Management emphasized ongoing integration efforts and cost discipline as key to driving future growth and profitability, while signaling confidence in the company’s strategic direction and market position.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Clean Energy Fuels rallies after Q2 beat but Jefferies downgrades on cautious outlook
data.description:
Clean Energy Fuels (NASDAQ:CLNE) +11.5% in Friday's trading after posting better than expected Q2 adjusted earnings and a nearly 5% Y/Y revenue increase to $102.6M, as the company sold 61.4M gallons of renewable natural gas in the period for a 7.5% gain.

Q2 adjusted EBITDA of $17.5M fell from $18.9M in the year-earlier quarter but topped analyst consensus estimates on strong volumes, partially offset by normalized pricing compared to Q1, prompting management to raise EBITDA guidance to $60M-$65M from $50M-$55M previously.

However, Clean Energy (NASDAQ:CLNE) was downgraded to Hold from Buy with a $2.20 price target at Jefferies, noting management's cautious tone on the X15N ramp, potentially pushing out the key growth driver.

Meanwhile, renewable natural gas is gaining traction in transit and refuse fleets, but adoption in long-haul heavy-duty trucking remains slow, largely due to an industry-wide downturn in new truck sales, according to Jefferies analysts including Julien Dumoulin-Smith, adding that third-party estimates point to a 12% Y/Y in Class 8 trucks in 2025.

The slower than expected ramp-up of the first set of dairy projects means near-term RNG production volumes and revenues for 2025 and 2026 will come in lower than originally expected, the analysts wrote.
